risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomly assigned to treatment group, stratified by race. Computer randomization occurred after the collection of baseline data." Comment: This explicitly states computer randomization with stratification by race, which is a clear, proper random allocation method and supports low risk of bias for sequence generation.

Quote: "Participants were randomly assigned to treatment group, stratified by race. Computer randomization occurred after the collection of baseline data." Comment: The article describes random assignment and computer randomization, but it does not state any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes). Therefore, it is not possible to tell whether the recruiter could foresee the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A prospective randomized controlled trial with two parallel interventions was employed." ... "A dietitian led all cohorts of the DSME intervention, and the same dietitian and a social worker with extensive training in mindful meditation co-led all cohorts of the MB-EAT intervention." ... "No information regarding mindful eating or meditation was presented." Comment: This was an open-label behavioral trial with different educational content and no indication that participants or intervention providers were blinded. Because the outcomes included self-reported diet-related behaviors, knowledge, self-efficacy, anxiety/depression, and mindfulness, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence outcomes; therefore performance bias is high.

Summary: The trial reports that the primary outcomes included both objective measures (weight determined using an electronic scale) and patient-reported questionnaires (e.g., Block Food Frequency Questionnaire, Beck Anxiety Inventory, Beck Depression Inventory, self-efficacy and mindfulness scales), but it does not state whether outcome assessors were blinded. Comment: Because the paper gives no explicit information on blinding of outcome assessment, and the outcomes are a mix of objective and subjective/self-reported measures, the risk of detection bias cannot be confidently judged.

Quote: "Thirty-two participants were randomized to MB-EAT-D; 27 received the allocated intervention and completed data collection. Thirty-six were randomized to SC; 25 received the allocated intervention and completed data collection. There was no significant difference in attrition between treatment groups (p > .05)..." Comment: Attrition was moderate but balanced across groups (5/32 vs 11/36 randomized not completing), and the authors report no significant difference in attrition between arms and no baseline differences between completers and non-completers. The analysis also used a mixed-effect ANOVA model with participants with at least two observed measures included, with few missing values (≤9%), which makes attrition bias unlikely.
